coredata.dc:title: Sodium hydroxide-mediated hydrogel of citrus pectin for preparation of fluorescent carbon dots for bioimaging
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2014 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Zhao, Xi Juan
Zhang, Wen Lin
Zhou, Zhi Qin
coredata.dc:description: The citrus process industry produces annually a huge amount of pomace, which is a rich source of citrus pectin. Here, we report the hydrogel of citrus pectin mediated by sodium hydroxide can be used to prepare fluorescent carbon dots (CDs). The introduction of hydrogel can not only make the temperature of the hydrothermal reaction down to 100°C, but also avoid visually carbonized precipitates in the synthesis process even up to 180°C. The as-synthesized CDs are well dispersed in water with an average size of 2.7nm and show cyan fluorescence with high photostability, good biocompatibility. Furthermore, the CDs can act as a potential fluorescent probe for cell imaging. Citrus pectin as a non-toxic carbonaceous precursor for preparation of fluorescent CDs provides a new approach for the efficient utilization of citrus germplasm in future.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Citrus pectin
Hydrogel
Fluorescent
Carbon dots

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Mechanisms of sodium hydroxide-mediated hydrogel formation and CDs production

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Optical properties of pectin and CDs. (A) UV–vis absorption spectra of pectin alone and pectin in the presence of NaOH. Inset: the optical images of pectin solution and the hydrogel of pectin formed in the presence of NaOH under daylight. (B) UV–vis absorption spectra of CDs prepared at 100°C, 120°C, 150°C and 180°C. (C) Optical images of CDs under daylight. (D) The corresponding images of CDs under 365nm UV lamp light.

Characteristic spectra of CDs obtained at 180°C. (A) UV–vis absorption spectrum (left), excitation spectrum (middle) and emission spectrum (right) of the CDs. Inset: the optical images of CDs under daylight and UV light, respectively. (B) The fluorescence spectra of the CDs excited with different excitation wavelengths from 360nm to 500nm. Inset: the normalized emission spectra of the CDs correspondingly.

Characterization of the CDs obtained at 180°C. (A) TEM image of the CDs (scale bar: 5nm), lower right inset: the size distribution of CDs based on statistics of the TEM image. (B) Wide XPS survey of the CDs. (C) High-resolution XPS C1s spectrum of the CDs. (D) Raman spectrum of the CDs. Laser wavelength: 532nm; slit: 100μm; hole: 400μm; acquisition time: 1s.

The practicality of CDs obtained at 180°C for cell imaging. (A) Biocompatibility testing of the CDs. (B and C) Fluorescent images of HEp-2 cells incubation with CDs under bright field and excitation at GFP457, respectively.

head.ce:title: Sodium hydroxide-mediated hydrogel of citrus pectin for preparation of fluorescent carbon dots for bioimaging

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Horticulture and Landscape Architecture, Southwest University, Chongqing 400716, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Horticulture and Landscape Architecture, Southwest University

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Horticulture Science for Southern Mountainous Regions, Ministry of Education, Chongqing 400715, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Horticulture Science for Southern Mountainous Regions, Ministry of Education

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Key Laboratory of Horticulture Science for Southern Mountainous Regions, Ministry of Education, College of Horticulture and Landscape Architecture, Horticulture Post-Doctoral Research Station, Southwest University, Chongqing, 400716, China. Tel.: +86 23 68250229; fax: +86 23 68251274.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The citrus process industry produces annually a huge amount of pomace, which is a rich source of citrus pectin. Here, we report the hydrogel of citrus pectin mediated by sodium hydroxide can be used to prepare fluorescent carbon dots (CDs). The introduction of hydrogel can not only make the temperature of the hydrothermal reaction down to 100°C, but also avoid visually carbonized precipitates in the synthesis process even up to 180°C. The as-synthesized CDs are well dispersed in water with an average size of 2.7nm and show cyan fluorescence with high photostability, good biocompatibility. Furthermore, the CDs can act as a potential fluorescent probe for cell imaging. Citrus pectin as a non-toxic carbonaceous precursor for preparation of fluorescent CDs provides a new approach for the efficient utilization of citrus germplasm in future.

ce:section.ce:para: Citrus is one of the most commonly consumed fruits in the world with an annual production of more than 100 million tons [1]. Citrus fruits can be consumed freshly or processed into dessert, juice and jam, and are good resources of vitamin C, folic acid, flavonoids, dietary fiber and many other health-promotion substances [2]. The citrus process industry produces annually a huge amount of pomace (usually 50% of the raw fruits). Besides the use as animal feed, the pomace can provide a rich source of pectin for food and other industries. Pectin is a naturally water-soluble polysaccharide with low production cost. Its backbone comprises linear chains of 1,4-linked α-D-galacturonic acid residues with some rhamnogalacturonic acid residue and α-L-rhamnopyranose by α-1-2 linkage, and contains many carboxyl groups and some methyl esters [3]. Owing to its biocompatibility, biodegradability and non-toxicity, pectin has been widely used in the pharmaceutical and food industries, e.g., acting as a carrier and coating material [4–6], as well as a gelling and thickening agent [7]. Nevertheless, the efficient use of citrus pomace has still been a problem unresolved. In this work, pectin is reported to be a non-toxic carbonaceous precursor for preparation of fluorescent carbon dots (CDs). The efficient use of citrus pectin will promote future germplasm utilization study of the genus Citrus L.

CDs have attracting considerable attention since the discovery of fluorescent carbon nanoparticles in 2004 [8]. Different from semiconductor quantum dots with the known toxicity and potential ecosystem hazard [9,10], CDs have their fascinating properties such as low cytotoxicity and good biocompatibility for fluorescent labeling [11]. To date, various routes for preparing CDs have been reported [12–14], e.g., laser ablation of graphite [15], electrochemical oxidation of multiwalled carbon nanotubes [16], hydrothermal reaction of some carbonaceous precursors such as C60 [17], Bombyx mori silk [18] and soy milk [19]. Among them, the hydrothermal route is popular owing to the simple reaction process and renewable carbonaceous precursors. And bio-precursors are particularly attractive considering the eco-friendly effects. Zhou et al. have synthesized fluorescent CDs from the hydrothermal carbonization of peach gum polysaccharide under the temperature of 180°C for 12h [20]. Such a high temperature can facilitate the carbonization of the precursors [21], but simultaneously lead to a lot of visually black particles in the products. While Sahu et al. reported that orange juice by the hydrothermal treatment of 120°C can produce highly luminescent CDs [22]. However, orange juice is a very nutritious drink, which is not suitable for mass production of CDs compared with the pectin that can be extracted from plenty of the residual pomace in the citrus processing. Here, we report a new kind of fluorescent CDs synthesized from the sodium hydroxide-mediated hydrogel of citrus pectin at relatively low temperatures down to 100°C. The involvement of hydrogel plays an important role in lowering the reaction temperature and can avoid visually carbonized precipitates even up to 180°C.

All reagents were obtained from commercial sources and used as received if no additional statement. Pectin from citrus peel (galacturonic acid ≥74.0%, dried basis) was purchased from Sigma–Aldrich Co. LLC. (USA). Ultrapure water (18.2MΩ) prepared with a Milli-Q system (Millipore, Bedford, MA, USA) was used throughout the experiments.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) were recorded with the Tecnai G2 F20 S-TWIN microscopy (FEI, USA). Elemental and functional groups analysis were made on an ESCALAB 250 X-ray photoelectron spectrometer and a FTIR-8400S Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (Shimadzu, Japan), respectively. Raman spectrum was taken on the Ag substrate using a LabRAM HR 800 Raman spectrometer (Horiba Jobin Yvon Inc., France). The zeta potential was recorded with Nano-ZS spectrometer (Malvern, UK) using the CDs after dialysis and in such case, the pH value of the CDs solution was determined to be about 7.0 with the pH test paper in order to make sure that residual NaOH and other small molecules have been detached. Fluorescence imaging was carried out with a DSU live-cell confocal microscope (Olympus, Japan) system. Absorption and fluorescence spectra were measured at room temperature with a U-3010 spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan) and an F-2500 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan), respectively.

The CDs were synthesized using pectin by a hydrothermal method. In a typical synthesis, 20mL Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave was cleaned in a bath of fresh aqua regia and rinsed thoroughly in H2O before using. Then, 2mL of 10g/L pectin and 2mL of 1mol/L NaOH were added to the autoclave. In such case, the hydrogels of pectin formed. And the autoclave was maintained at 100°C, 120°C, 150°C or 180°C for 2h. After the autoclave cooled down naturally, a homogeneously light brown solution was obtained, indicating the formation of CDs. Next, the light brown solution was centrifuged at 15,000rpm for 10min to get the upper solution. Through a dialysis membrane (1000 MWCO), residual NaOH and other small molecules will be detached. The obtained CDs solution was then freeze-dried under vacuum in order to get solid powder for further use.

As for the contrast experiments, there is no NaOH-mediated hydrogel in the synthesis procedure. The details are shown as below. 2mL of 10g/L pectin and 2mL of H2O were added to the 20mL autoclave. After mixing thoroughly, the autoclave was maintained at 100°C, 120°C, 150°C or 180°C for 2h. When the autoclave cooled down naturally, we can obtain the corresponding products with different states.

The HEp-2 cells (2×105 cells/mL) in RPMI 1640 culture medium supplemented with 10% fetal bovine serum (FBS) were added to each well of a 96-well plate (100μL per well). The cells were first cultured for 24h in an incubator (37°C, 5% CO2), and for another 24h, the culture medium was replaced with 100μL of RPMI 1640 (2% FBS) containing 10μL of the CDs prepared at 180°C with different concentrations (0, 0.01, 0.05, 0.1, 0.2, 0.5, 1mg/mL). Cells cultured in the medium without adding CDs were taken as control. After that, the cells were washed with PBS buffer solution twice, and then, 90μL PBS buffer and 10μL of CCK-8 solution were added to each well and incubated for another 30min. The optical density (OD) of the mixture was measured at 450nm with a Microplate Reader Model. The cell viability was estimated by the ratio of absorbance of the cells incubated with CDs to that of the cells incubated with the culture medium only.

The HEp-2 cells in RPMI 1640 culture medium containing 10% fetal bovine serum were added to each well of a 24-well plate (300μL per well). The cells were first cultured for 24h in an incubator (37°C, 5% CO2), and for another 24h after the culture medium was replaced with 270μL of RPMI 1640 culture medium containing 30μL CDs obtained at 180°C (1mg/mL). After that, followed removing the culture medium, each well was washed with PBS buffer for three times. Then the cells were fixed with 4% paraformaldehyde for 30min, and mounted with glycerol on microscope slides for imaging.

The aqueous solution of pectin shows a distinct absorption band centered at 286nm with a shoulder peak at around 350nm (Fig. 1A). Here, when an appropriate amount of the aqueous solution of NaOH is added to the pectin solution, hydrogel of pectin forms immediately accompanied with the color change to light-green. However, the absorption spectrum of pectin has no significant changes as shown in Fig. 1A. After the hydrogel was transferred to Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave maintaining at 100°C, 120°C, 150°C or 180°C for 2h, respectively, the hydrogel will be destroyed and the color of the product turns yellow or light brown to some extent (Fig. 1C), indicating the formation of CDs. Also, the absorption spectra have been changed. The featured absorption peak at 286nm disappeared and a new one centered at 260nm appeared gradually with increasing the temperature (Fig. 1B). Under 365nm UV lamp light, the CDs can exhibit varying degrees of cyan fluorescence emission. And the higher the temperature, the stronger the fluorescence intensity will be (Fig. 1D). Since either pectin or the hydrogel has no fluorescence emission, the emission comes from the new generated CDs. Furthermore, the fluorescence spectra of the corresponding CDs are dependent of the excitation wavelength (Fig. 2 and Supporting Information, Figs. S1–S3). This excitation-dependent emission property is in accordance with that of CDs in previous reports. As shown in Fig. 2, the CDs obtained at 180°C as an example have a broad UV absorption band with a featured peak at 260nm and exhibit strong emission centered at around 460nm when excited at 360nm. The emission peaks can shift from 460nm to 554nm with the increase of the excitation wavelength. And the quantum yield is calculated to be approximately 1.1% with quinine sulfate as the reference, which is comparable to that of the CDs obtained from natural gas soot (0.43%) as reported [13].

However, if no hydrogel is mediated in the synthesis procedure, no fluorescent CDs can be formed from pectin under the temperature of 100°C and 120°C for 2h (Supporting Information, Fig. S4). The color of the pectin solution has no changes, and under 365nm UV lamp light, the aqueous solution shows very weak fluorescence emission and there is no distinct excitation-dependent emission property of CDs as shown in the fluorescence spectra (Supporting Information, Fig. S5). When the temperature reaches 150°C, the product becomes brown with a lot of visible black precipitates, suggesting the pectin has been carbonized (Supporting Information, Fig. S4). After getting rid of the precipitates, the upper solution shows blue fluorescence (Supporting Information, Fig. S5). In fact, carbonization is more serious under 180°C and the fluorescence of the upper solution is decreased (Supporting Information, Fig. S5). So, sodium hydroxide-mediated hydrogel can make the fluorescent CDs synthesized under the temperatures down to 100°C and also can avoid visually carbonized precipitates even up to 180°C.

The TEM image reveals that the as-prepared CDs well separate from each other (Fig. 3A and Supporting Information, Fig. S6). The corresponding particle size distribution histogram shows that the CDs have a narrow size distribution with an average size of 2.7nm. Composition and element analysis of the synthesized CDs were performed by XPS as shown in Fig. 3B and C. The wide scan XPS spectrum has three strong peaks ascribed to sodium, oxygen and carbon in turn. The sodium comes from NaOH added in the synthesis process of CDs. In addition, there is a very weak peak at about 400.0eV, indicating the presence of nitrogen. Since pectin is a polysaccharide with chemical composition of C, H and O, the nitrogen might come from impurities. So, the peak at 285.7eV in the C1s spectrum can be attributed to CN band [18]. And the other three peaks at 284.6, 286.7 and 288.1eV in Fig. 3C are attributed to the CC, CO, and CO bands, respectively [18]. FTIR spectra show that the CDs obtained at 100°C, 120°C, 150°C and 180°C have almost the same characteristic absorption bands, indicating that the four CDs have the same functional groups (Supporting Information, Fig. S7). The characteristic absorption bands of OH stretching vibrations at 3422cm−1, CO stretching vibrations at 1643cm−1, CC stretching vibrations at 1600cm−1, and CH bending vibrations at 1447cm−1 suggest that functional groups such as COOH and OH were attached on the surface of CDs (Supporting Information, Fig. S7). The zeta potential of the CDs was −41.3±1.1mV, indicating the surface was negatively charged due to the carboxylic acid groups. Raman spectrum taken on the Ag substrate was used to identify the state of carbon. A narrow G-line at around 1604cm−1 and a broad D-line at around 1370cm−1 indicated the presence of both sp2 carbon and sp3 hybridized carbon in the CDs (Fig. 3D).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanisms of sodium hydroxide-mediated hydrogel formation and CDs production
ce:section.ce:para: Usually, pectin is able to form hydrogel depending on several parameters such as saccharose, low pH values and polyvalent cations. Under alkaline conditions such as in the presence of sodium hydroxide, formation of the pectin hydrogel has hardly been reported before. It is known that under the alkaline conditions, polysaccharides will undergo various transformations such as alkaline hydrolysis, depolymerization, and oxidizing destruction [23]. And Zahran et al. indicated that these processes can take place for pectin to give galacturonic acid and some monosaccharides, e.g., galactose, arabinose, rhamnose, glucose, and mannose [24]. In this work, once the appropriate amount of NaOH was added to the pectin solution, hydrogel forms immediately. Since there are no divalent or trivalent metal ions, the formation of hydrogel is mainly attributed to the saccharides produced by pectin in the alkaline medium. Saccharides can serve as the dehydrating agent and promote the formation of hydrogen bonds between pectin molecules through competing with pectin to bind water. And further, micelles and three-dimensional network structures of pectin will form, finally leading to the formation of hydrogel. In fact, the sodium hydroxide-mediated hydrogel here is a multicomponent carbohydrate owing to the transformations of a part amount of pectin, which is different from the hydrogel of pectin formed in the other media.

Compared to the pectin solution alone, the sodium hydroxide-mediated hydrogel of pectin is easier to produce fluorescent CDs under lower temperatures down to 100°C. It is obvious that the hydrogel plays an important role. In our opinion, the hydrogel decreased some complex chemical reactions that pectin should undergo under hydrothermal conditions such as structure destruction, polymerization and carbonization [25]. In the hydrogel, there are already network structures of pectin mixed with the saccharides produced by pectin in the presence of sodium hydroxide. During the hydrothermal treatment, the hydrogel can be broken down into individual small nanoparticles yielding fluorescent CDs.

The obtained CDs show excellent photostability, and no sign of photobleaching was observed even after 60min of continuous excitation at 370nm with the Xe lamp (Supporting Information, Fig. S8). Furthermore, the cytotoxicity of the CDs and their fluorescent imaging were evaluated by using HEp-2 cells as a sample. As shown in Fig. 4A, the average cell viability was greater than 97% at all these tested doses of CDs ranging from 0 to 1.0mg/mL. Even after the incubation time increased up to 24h, the cell proliferation was not affected, indicating the CDs are very biocompatible. Fig. 4B and C displays that the CDs possess good fluorescent signals for cell imaging, and are very likely to enter the cells through the cell membrane via endocytosis. The CDs can be located in the cell membrane and cytoplasm, lightening the cells. A control experiment of cells without incubation of CDs was also performed, showing that no obvious autofluorescence and negligible endogenous fluorescence can be observed under the same conditions (Supporting Information, Fig. S9). These results indicate that the CDs can serve as a potential fluorescent probe for cell imaging.

In this study, we report that sodium hydroxide can mediate the hydrogel formation of citrus pectin, which is different from commonly gel-forming conditions of pectin. And through the involvement of the hydrogel, we can successfully synthesize fluorescent CDs at low temperatures down to 100°C by using the hydrothermal treatment. The CDs have high photostability, good biocompatibility and a potential use for fluorescent imaging in cells. Our study suggests a new approach for efficient use of citrus pectin in future, and has significance for the disposal of pomace produced in the citrus process industry.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (No. 2014M552304), Chongqing Postdoctoral Science Foundation (Xm2014005), the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (XDJK2014A014), and the Program for Chongqing Innovation Team of University (KJTD201333).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.colsurfb.2014.09.048.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following are the supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: The Chemistry and Technology of Pectin
